title: Trie
description: A specialized tree for strings: sharing common prefixes across an unordered_map of children, enabling fast insert, search, and prefix queries.
objectives: Understand a trie as a tree specialized for storing strings by shared prefix
Implement insert and search on a trie
Understand why a trie beats a hash set for prefix-based lookups

sections.text: 1. Recap: A Specialized Tree for Strings

Binary Search Trees branched based on comparing whole values. A trie (pronounced "try," from re-TRIE-val) branches character by character, letting words that share a prefix share the same path through the tree.
A trie is a tree where each edge represents one character, and words sharing a common prefix share the same path from the root — with a marker on any node where a complete word ends.

Unlike TreeNode's fixed left/right, a trie node can have any number of children — one per possible next character — which is exactly why unordered_map fits here: it maps each character to the node representing "the path so far, plus this character."

sections.title: Why these three results differ
points: search("cat") is true — "cat" was fully inserted, and its final node ('t') is marked isEndOfWord
search("ca") is false — the path c→a exists (it's a prefix of both "cat" and "car"), but 'a' was never marked as the end of a complete word
startsWith("ca") is true — it only checks whether the PATH exists, not whether anything is marked as a complete word there
"cat" and "car" share the exact same c→a path in the trie — only the branches after 'a' (to 't' and to 'r') diverge

sections.text: 5. Why a Trie Beats a Hash Set for Prefix Queries

Recall unordered_set: checking whether an exact word exists is O(1) average — but checking whether ANY word starts with a given prefix would require scanning every stored word, O(n). A trie answers startsWith() in O(k), where k is just the prefix's length, completely independent of how many words are stored — exactly why tries power autocomplete and spell-check features.

sections.title: A phone keypad's predictive text, narrowing down as you type
sections.text: Typing 'c', then 'a' on a predictive keyboard narrows suggestions to only words starting with "ca" — the keyboard doesn't rescan every word in the dictionary on each keystroke; it's already sitting at the right branch, ready to suggest whatever comes next. That's a trie: each typed character moves you one step further down an already-organized path.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing search() and startsWith() — search() requires isEndOfWord to be true at the final node; startsWith() only requires the path to exist.
Forgetting to mark isEndOfWord = true after inserting a word — without it, search() would incorrectly return false even for a fully inserted word.
Assuming a trie always uses less memory than a hash set — a trie can use MORE memory than storing raw strings, trading memory for prefix-query speed.
Forgetting that words sharing a prefix share nodes — inserting both "cat" and "car" doesn't create two separate 'c' and 'a' nodes; they reuse the same path.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a trie?
questions.answer: A tree specialized for storing strings, where each edge represents one character and words sharing a common prefix share the same path from the root, with a marker at nodes where a complete word ends.

questions.question: What is the difference between search() and startsWith() in a trie?
questions.answer: search() requires the final node reached to be marked isEndOfWord (a complete word was inserted); startsWith() only requires that the character path exists, regardless of whether it marks a complete word.

questions.question: Why is a trie better suited than a hash set for prefix queries?
questions.answer: A trie answers 'does any word start with this prefix' in time proportional only to the prefix's length, while a hash set would need to scan every stored word to answer the same question.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A trie is a tree specialized for strings: each edge represents one character, so words sharing a common prefix — like "cat" and "car" — share the same path through the tree, using an unordered_map<char, TrieNode*> at each node to branch by next character. isEndOfWord marks where a complete word actually ends, which is why search("ca") can be false even though startsWith("ca") is true — the path exists as a shared prefix, but nothing was inserted ending exactly there. This structure is why tries beat a hash set for prefix-based queries like autocomplete: startsWith() only costs time proportional to the prefix length, not the number of stored words.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Trie
sections.text: Trie = predictive text, one keystroke narrows the branch. Shared prefixes share the same path. isEndOfWord = a real word ends HERE, not just a prefix passing through.

sections.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
sections.problemStatement: Implement a trie with insert(word), search(word), and startsWith(prefix) methods.

examples.input: insert("apple"); search("apple"); search("app"); startsWith("app"); insert("app"); search("app")

constraints: word and prefix consist of lowercase English letters
hints: This is exactly this lesson's TrieNode + unordered_map<char, TrieNode*> structure — insert walks/creates nodes per character, marking isEndOfWord at the last one.
search and startsWith share almost identical logic — the only difference is search additionally requires isEndOfWord to be true at the final node.
A single private helper that walks to the node for a given string (or returns nullptr if the path breaks) can back both search() and startsWith().

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all inserted words in a vector and linearly scan it for search/startsWith — no trie structure at all.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) per search/startsWith call, where n is the number of stored words times word length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Real trie: each node branches by character via unordered_map<char, TrieNode*>, with isEndOfWord marking complete words.

sections.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
sections.problemStatement: Design a data structure that supports addWord(word) and search(word), where search may contain '.' characters that match any single letter.

examples.input: addWord("bad"); addWord("dad"); addWord("mad"); search("pad"); search("bad"); search(".ad"); search("b..")

constraints: word consists of lowercase English letters and possibly '.'
hints: addWord is identical to LC 208's insert() — the wildcard only affects search.
'.' means 'try every child at this position' — this turns a simple loop into a DFS/backtracking search over the trie.
Write a recursive helper(node, word, index): on '.', recurse into every entry in node->children; on a normal character, recurse into just that one child (or fail if it doesn't exist).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all added words in a vector; for each search, compare position-by-position against every stored word, treating '.' as a wildcard match.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * L) per search, where n is the number of stored words and L is word length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Trie for addWord, with a recursive DFS search that branches into every child on a '.' wildcard.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(26^d) worst case per search, where d is the number of '.' characters; O(L) when there are none
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(total characters stored), plus O(L) recursion depth

sections.title: Replace Words
sections.problemStatement: Given a dictionary of root words and a sentence, replace every word in the sentence that has a root in the dictionary with its shortest matching root.

examples.input: dictionary = ["cat","bat","rat"], sentence = "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
examples.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

hints: Insert every root into a trie exactly like LC 208's insert().
For each word in the sentence, walk the trie character by character; the moment you hit a node with isEndOfWord true, stop — that's the shortest root.
If the walk falls off the trie (no matching child) or never hits isEndOfWord, the word keeps its original form.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each sentence word, check every dictionary root as a literal prefix and keep the shortest match.
approaches.output: the cat was rat by the bat

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Insert all roots into a trie, then for each sentence word walk the trie until isEndOfWord is hit or the path breaks.
approaches.output: the cat was rat by the bat
approaches.timeComplexity: O(total root characters + total sentence characters)
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(total root characters) for the trie

sections.title: Map Sum Pairs
sections.problemStatement: Implement insert(key, val) and sum(prefix), where sum returns the total of all values whose key starts with the given prefix. Inserting an existing key overwrites (not adds to) its value.
hints: The overwrite rule means you must track each key's current value separately (e.g. an unordered_map<string,int>) to compute the delta when a key is re-inserted.
Store a running sum at EVERY trie node along a key's path, not just at isEndOfWord — that running sum is exactly what a prefix query needs.
On insert, add (newVal - oldVal) to every node's sum along the path; on sum(prefix), just walk to that node and read its stored sum directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store key-value pairs in an unordered_map; for sum(prefix), scan every key checking for the prefix and total matching values.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(keys * key length) per sum() call

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Trie storing a running prefix-sum at every node, updated by the value delta on each insert/overwrite.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(key length) per insert; O(prefix length) per sum()
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(total key characters) for the trie
